hints: Attach an indicator to each letter: 1 if it lands at the right firm.
Each letter is correct with probability 1/X; there are X of them.
E = X·(1/X) = 1 (true for any X).
solution: Let $I_i=1$ if letter $i$ goes to its correct firm. Under a uniform random assignment $P(I_i=1)=\tfrac1X$, and by linearity $\mathbb{E}[\sum_i I_i]=X\cdot\tfrac1X=1.$ (The expected number of fixed points of a random permutation is always $1$.)

hints: Each digit lands in each of the 9 positions in 8! of the numbers.
solution:
Each digit occupies each of the $9$ positions in exactly $8!$ of the permutations, so
$$\text{sum}=8!\,(1+2+\dots+9)\,\underbrace{111111111}_{(10^9-1)/9}=8!\cdot45\cdot\frac{10^9-1}{9}=5\cdot 8!\,(10^9-1).$$
Thus $k=5,\,a=8,\,b=9$ and $abk=360.$

hints: Outcomes: both blue (1/4) Alice +1; both red (1/4) Alice +3; else (1/2) Bob +2.
solution:
Each sibling shows blue/red with probability $\tfrac12$, independently. So both-blue and both-red each have probability $\tfrac14$, and Bob wins otherwise (prob $\tfrac12$).

Alice's payoff is $1,3,0$ with probabilities $\tfrac14,\tfrac14,\tfrac12$: $\mathbb{E}=1$, $\mathbb{E}[A^2]=\tfrac{1+9}{4}=2.5$, $\mathrm{Var}=1.5$, so $A_r=\tfrac{1}{1.5}=\tfrac23$. Bob's payoff is $2$ w.p. $\tfrac12$ else $0$: $\mathbb{E}=1$, $\mathrm{Var}=2-1=1$, so $B_r=1$. Hence $B_r-A_r=1-\tfrac23=\tfrac13.$

hints: A_n = x x^T is rank 1, so its only nonzero eigenvalue equals the trace = xᵀx.
So λ_n/n^k → finite nonzero when k=3.
solution: $A_n=x_nx_n^T$ has rank $1$, so its single nonzero eigenvalue equals $\mathrm{tr}(A_n)=x_n^Tx_n=\sum_{i=1}^n i^2=\dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}\sim\dfrac{n^3}{3}.$ Therefore $\lambda_n/n^k$ converges to a finite nonzero limit exactly for $k=3.$

hints: Condition on whether the wheel reveals the door.
Reveals (3/5): you win for sure. No info (2/5): guess 1/3.
solution: Condition on the wheel: with probability $\tfrac35$ it reveals the door (you win for sure); with probability $\tfrac25$ you guess uniformly among $3$ (win $\tfrac13$). So $P=\tfrac35\cdot1+\tfrac25\cdot\tfrac13=\tfrac9{15}+\tfrac2{15}=\tfrac{11}{15}.$

hints: 122577 has repeated digits (two 2s, two 7s). Two tiles fall (C(6,2)=15 equally likely pairs).
If the two fallen tiles are identical, any reinsertion restores it (prob 1); otherwise only 1 of 2 orders works (prob 1/2).
Same-digit pairs: {the two 2s},{the two 7s}=2; rest 13. P=(2·1+13·½)/15=17/30.
solution:
The number $122577$ has digits $\{1,2,2,5,7,7\}$. Two positions fall (uniformly among $\binom62=15$ pairs), and he reinserts the two tiles in one of $2$ orders. If the two fallen tiles show the same digit, either order restores the number (prob $1$); if different, only one order works (prob $\tfrac12$). The same-digit pairs are the two $2$'s and the two $7$'s ($2$ pairs); the other $13$ are different. So
$$P=\frac{2\cdot1+13\cdot\tfrac12}{15}=\frac{8.5}{15}=\frac{17}{30}.$$

hints: Given first roll x, compare expected payout of betting 'second ≥ x' vs 'second < x'.
Bet-lower payout ∝ Σ_{y<x} y = (x−1)x/2; bet-higher ∝ Σ_{y≥x} y = 5050 − (x−1)x/2.
Guess lower once (x−1)x > 5050 ⇒ x ≥ 72.
solution: After the first roll $x$, betting 'second $\ge x$' has expected payout $\propto\sum_{y=x}^{100}y=5050-\tfrac{(x-1)x}{2}$, while betting 'second $<x$' has $\propto\sum_{y=1}^{x-1}y=\tfrac{(x-1)x}{2}$. You guess lower once the latter is bigger: $(x-1)x>5050$, i.e. $x^2-x-5050>0\Rightarrow x>\tfrac{1+\sqrt{20201}}{2}\approx71.6$. The smallest such integer is $t=72.$

hints: Complement: their triangles are disjoint only if Bob picks exactly the 3 points Alice didn't.
Given Alice's 3 points, Bob has C(6,3)=20 choices, only 1 fully disjoint.
solution: Use the complement. Given Alice's $3$ points, the triangles are disjoint only if Bob picks exactly the other $3$ points — $1$ of his $\binom63=20$ equally likely choices. So $P(\text{disjoint})=\tfrac1{20}$ and $P(\text{share a vertex})=\tfrac{19}{20}.$

hints: Track states up to symmetry: I (identity) and P (a diagonal-1 matrix with one off-diagonal flipped to 1). Both have det=1.
From I: 2/4 flips give a singular matrix, 2/4 go to P. From P: 3/4 give singular, 1/4 returns to I.
solution:
From $I_2$, flipping a diagonal $1\to0$ makes it singular (2 of 4 moves); the other 2 moves set an off-diagonal to $1$, reaching a state $P$ (e.g. $\begin{smallmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{smallmatrix}$), still $\det=1$. From such a $P$, $3$ of the $4$ flips give $\det=0$ and $1$ returns to $I$. Let $E_I,E_P$ be expected steps:
$$E_I=1+\tfrac12E_P,\qquad E_P=1+\tfrac14E_I.$$
Substituting, $E_I=1+\tfrac12(1+\tfrac14E_I)=\tfrac32+\tfrac18E_I\Rightarrow E_I=\tfrac{12}{7}.$

hints: Let V be the game value; rerolling (cost $1) is worth V−1, so keep a roll k iff k ≥ V−1.
Guess the threshold and solve self-consistently.
solution:
Let $V$ be the value of the game. A reroll costs $\$1$ and returns you to the same situation, so its value is $V-1$; you keep a roll $k$ iff $k\ge V-1$. Solving self-consistently with threshold $4$ (reroll on $1,2,3$):
$$V=\tfrac16\big[3(V-1)+(4+5+6)\big]=\tfrac{V}{2}+2\;\Rightarrow\;V=4.$$
This is consistent (reroll value $3$, so keep $4,5,6$), giving expected payout $\$4.$

hints: Choose the 4 fed children, then distribute 12 units among them with each ≥1.
C(10,4)=210; positive solutions of x₁+…+x₄=12 ⇒ C(11,3)=165.
solution: Choose which $4$ children are fed: $\binom{10}{4}=210$. Then distribute $12$ units among them with each $\ge1$: setting $y_i=x_i-1$ gives $\sum y_i=8$, with $\binom{8+3}{3}=\binom{11}{3}=165$ solutions. Total $=210\cdot165=34650.$

hints: There are 15 ways to pair {1,…,6} into three pairs; compute each sum of products.
List the resulting sums and remove duplicates.
The distinct sums are {28,29,31,32,35,37,40,41,44} — 9 of them.
solution: There are $15$ perfect matchings of $\{1,\dots,6\}$ into three pairs. Computing $ab+cd+ef$ for each yields the values $44,41,40,41,37,35,40,32,31,35,31,29,32,29,28$. The distinct sums are $\{28,29,31,32,35,37,40,41,44\}$, so there are $9$ unique sums.
